did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained on 11,444 dermoscopic images to classify skin lesions into five diagnostic categories. The CNN's predictions were combined with those of 112 dermatologists using a gradient boosting method (XGBoost) to achieve superior skin cancer classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'Original Research' on the first page. It presents original experimental results from a study involving 112 dermatologists and a CNN classifying 300 biopsy-verified skin lesions, including specific methods, statistical analysis (McNemar-tests), and quantitative findings (e.g., 82.95% accuracy for the fusion method).

title: Superior skin cancer classification by the combination of human and artificial intelligence
brief_summary: This study investigated whether combining human and artificial intelligence improves skin cancer classification compared to individual classifiers. A convolutional neural network (CNN) was trained on 11,444 dermoscopic images from the ISIC archive to classify lesions into five diagnostic categories. The CNN's predictions were then combined with independent diagnoses from 112 dermatologists using a gradient boosting method (XGBoost) that incorporated decision certainty. The fused classifier achieved an accuracy of 82.95% in the multiclass task, outperforming the best individual classifier (the CNN at 81.59%). For binary classification of benign versus malignant lesions, the combined approach also demonstrated superior sensitivity (89%) compared to the CNN alone (86.1%). The findings indicate that integrating human and artificial intelligence yields superior diagnostic results over either system operating independently.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five diagnostic categories used in the multiclass classification task. Part of the malignant group in the binary classification task.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Actinic Keratosis / Squamous Cell Carcinoma / Bowen's Disease
lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped into a single diagnostic category (class a) for the multiclass task. Part of the malignant group in the binary classification task.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Benign Keratosis / Seborrheic Keratosis / Solar Lentigo / Lichen Planus-like Keratosis
lesion_types_studied.details: Grouped into a single diagnostic category (class k) for the multiclass task. Part of the benign group in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Five diagnostic categories of skin lesions (multiclass) and benign versus malignant status (binary)
all_task_types.details: The study performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into five diagnostic categories (actinic keratosis/Bowen's disease/squamous cell carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis/seborrheic keratosis/solar lentigo/lichen planus-like keratosis, melanocytic nevi, and melanoma) and binary classification of lesions as benign or malignant. The CNN outputs a five-dimensional probability distribution over the classes, and the fusion method combines these with dermatologist diagnoses to predict discrete diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive / HAM10000 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public archive containing dermoscopic images of heterogeneous populations. The study used a cleaned subset of the HAM10000 dataset (one image per lesion) supplemented with additional images from the ISIC archive to create the training data.
classes_used: class a: actinic keratosis, intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease, squamous cell carcinoma
class k: benign keratosis, seborrhoeic keratosis, solar lentigo, lichen planus-like keratosis

use.training: Used to train the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The final training set consisted of 12,336 images (including data augmentation) derived from 11,444 unique images (6,390 biopsy-verified).
use.other: The test set for the reader study consisted of 300 biopsy-verified images randomly selected from the available biopsy-verified image set within the HAM10000 Dataset.

datasets_used.name: Reader Study Test Set
datasets_used.description: A subset of 300 biopsy-verified skin lesion images from the HAM10000 Dataset, randomly selected to prevent selection bias. These images were used for the independent classification tasks by dermatologists and the CNN.
classes_used: class a: actinic keratosis, intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease, squamous cell carcinoma
class k: benign keratosis, seborrhoeic keratosis, solar lentigo, lichen planus-like keratosis

use.testing: Used as the test set for the independent classification tasks performed by 112 dermatologists and the trained CNN. The images were split into six questionnaires of 50 images each.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To statistically compare the accuracy of the multiclass problem as well as the sensitivity, specificity, and overall classification of the binary problem between classifiers.
To compare the performance of the classifiers in the form of 2x2 tables.
To determine statistical significance for the McNemar-tests.
Used within the 'RandomizedSearchCV' function in scikit-learn to tune hyperparameters of the XGBoost algorithm.
To present results for the XGBoost classifier, which contains stochastic components, thereby preventing cherry picking.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in performance (accuracy for multiclass task; sensitivity, specificity, and overall classification for binary task) between the compared classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a difference in performance between the compared classifiers.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical significance was determined by a p-value threshold of less than 0.05. The study used these tests to compare the accuracy of the multiclass problem and the sensitivity, specificity, and overall classification of the binary problem between the dermatologists, the CNN, and the fusion method.

multiclass_classification.notes: The fusion method achieved an accuracy 1.36% higher than the CNN. The difference was not statistically significant. Results for the fusion method were averaged over 20 runs.

binary_classification_benign_vs_malignan.notes: The increase in sensitivity for the fusion method compared to the CNN was statistically significant (p < 0.05). At an equal sensitivity of 89%, the CNN achieved a specificity of only 81.5%.

per_questionnaire_multiclass_accuracy.notes: Physician accuracy varied significantly by group size and composition. The CNN consistently outperformed individual dermatologist groups.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported in the provided text, though the model family is ResNet50.

architecture_details.justification: Selected based on good previous experience with ResNet50 architecture for skin lesion classification and confirmation by Kassani et al. [21].

training_details.dataset_size: 12,336 images (derived from 11,444 unique images after cleaning and augmentation)
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Class n down-sampled by a factor of 2; other classes up-sampled by a factor of 3 via data augmentation.
training_details.hyperparameters: Not reported for CNN; XGBoost hyperparameters tuned via RandomizedSearchCV with cross-validation.

classes: class a: actinic keratosis, intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease, squamous cell carcinoma
class k: benign keratosis, seborrhoeic keratosis, solar lentigo, lichen planus-like keratosis

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning: Removed duplicate images showing the same lesion from different magnifications and angles to ensure only one image per lesion was used.
Data augmentation: Applied to handle class imbalance (down-sampling class n by a factor of 2; up-sampling other classes by a factor of 3).
Normalization of dermatologist certainty: Dermatologist certainty scores (0-10) were divided by 10 to normalize them into a [0,1] probability range for the fusion algorithm.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Class balancing via sampling and augmentation
data_augmentation_techniques.details: To handle class imbalance, class n (melanocytic nevi) was down-sampled by a factor of 2. Other classes (class a, b, k, m) were up-sampled by a factor of 3 via data augmentation. The final training set consisted of 12,336 images (585 class a, 910 class b, 3101 class k, 4219 class n, 3521 class m).

conclusions: The combination of human and artificial intelligence achieves superior diagnostic results compared to the independent performance of either system alone.
In the multiclass classification task, the fusion method achieved an accuracy of 82.95%, which was 1.36% higher than the best individual classifier (the CNN at 81.59%).

In the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), the fusion method demonstrated superior sensitivity (89%) compared to the CNN (86.1%), with the difference being statistically significant (p < 0.05).

While the specificity of the combined classifier decreased (84%) compared to the CNN alone (89.2%), the CNN achieved a lower specificity (81.5%) when adjusted to match the fusion method's sensitivity of 89%.
The study supports the concept that team-based diagnoses, combining human and machine intelligence, are more accurate than individual diagnoses in clinical settings.
important_remarks: The study used a test set consisting exclusively of biopsy-verified images, which introduces a selection bias towards difficult-to-diagnose lesions and may explain the relatively low accuracy of the participating dermatologists.

The CNN and the fusion method were trained and validated on data from the same database (ISIC/HAM10000), limiting the assessment of true external generalizability; performance may degrade on entirely external datasets.

The improvement in multiclass accuracy for the fusion method (82.95%) over the CNN alone (81.59%) was not statistically significant (p >= 0.05 implied by lack of significance statement for this specific comparison in the abstract/results context, though sensitivity was significant).

The fusion method achieved superior sensitivity (89%) compared to the CNN (86.1%) for binary classification, a difference that was statistically significant (p < 0.05), but at the cost of reduced specificity (84% vs 89.2%).

Dermatologist certainty was self-reported on a 0-10 scale and normalized to [0,1]; the remaining probability mass for non-selected classes was assumed to be uniformly distributed, which is a simplification of actual diagnostic uncertainty.

The study population was limited to dermatologists from 13 German university hospitals, potentially limiting the generalizability of the human classifier performance to other clinical settings or regions.

The CNN architecture (ResNet50) was selected based on prior experience rather than extensive architectural search, and specific hyperparameters for the CNN training were not reported in the main text.
Results for the XGBoost fusion model were averaged over 20 runs to mitigate stochasticity and prevent cherry-picking, enhancing the robustness of the reported fusion metrics.
